coredata.dc:title: Visual detection of G-quadruplex with mushroom derived highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Bhavini
Kumari, Rekha
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: G-quadruplexes are secondary DNA structures frequently found in telomeres and DNA sequences related to gene regulation, etc. Herein, we report a label free, sensitive and visually detectable fluorescence based biosensing platform for the detection of G-quadruplexes in DNA. Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dot (CD) with enriched functional groups was synthesized from Ganoderma lucidum, an oriental fungus by using a green hydrothermal process. Noncovalent functionalization of CD was done with Hemin where Hemin/CD conjugate forms a quenched union. However, in presence of DNA sequences that contain G-quadruplexes, the fluorescence of CD is restored selectively. The fluorescence restoration of CD is attributed to the stripping of Hemin from Hemin/CD conjugate that preferably slide into the G quadruplexes leaving CD. This increase in CD fluorescence is easily detectable under hand-held UV light without any other instrumental intervention for G-quadruplex containing DNA only and not in any other DNA samples. Also, such selective fluorescent recovery was not observed with chemically synthesized CD that lack hydroxyl functionality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hemin
G-quadruplex
Carbon dot
Ganoderma lucidum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of G quadruplex and characterization
Optical properties of CD and Hemin/CD conjugate
Evaluation of thermodynamic parameters for interaction between CD and Hemin as well as sequence 1 (VEGF) and Hemin
Detection of G-quadruplex with Hemin/CD conjugate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of G quadruplex detection by novel Hemin/CD conjugate.
Characterization of CD synthesized from Ganoderma lucidum A. XRD spectra B. HRTEM image C. FTIR spectra D. ZETA potential.
A. Whole survey XPS spectrum of CD synthesized from Ganoderma lucidum. B. C1s XPS spectrum C. N1s XPS spectrum and D. O1s XPS spectrum of CD.

A. UV–vis spectra of CD B. Fluorescence spectra of CD at different excitation wavelength C. Fluorescence quenching of CD with different concentration of Hemin D. Fluorescence lifetime of CD and Hemin/CD conjugate.
A. ITC profile of Hemin binding with CD 4B. ITC profile for Hemin binding with VEGF sequence.

A. Digital image of CD (left), Hemin quenched CD (middle) and fluorescence recovery of CD with G-Quadruplex in sequence 1 (right) B. Gradual increase in fluorescence intensity of CD in Hemin/CD conjugate with increasing amount of G quadruplex (sequence 1) C. Plot of (F–F0) versus concentration of G Quadruplex (C, μmol L−1) for LOD calculation D. Fluorescence lifetime of Hemin/CD Conjugate and Hemin/CD conjugate with G-quadruplex.
Sequences of oligonucleotide used for G quadruplex preparation.

head.ce:title: Visual detection of G-quadruplex with mushroom derived highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, IIT Patna, Bihta, Patna, 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry A. N. College, Patna, 800013, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry A. N. College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: G-quadruplexes are secondary DNA structures frequently found in telomeres and DNA sequences related to gene regulation, etc. Herein, we report a label free, sensitive and visually detectable fluorescence based biosensing platform for the detection of G-quadruplexes in DNA. Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dot (CD) with enriched functional groups was synthesized from Ganoderma lucidum, an oriental fungus by using a green hydrothermal process. Noncovalent functionalization of CD was done with Hemin where Hemin/CD conjugate forms a quenched union. However, in presence of DNA sequences that contain G-quadruplexes, the fluorescence of CD is restored selectively. The fluorescence restoration of CD is attributed to the stripping of Hemin from Hemin/CD conjugate that preferably slide into the G quadruplexes leaving CD. This increase in CD fluorescence is easily detectable under hand-held UV light without any other instrumental intervention for G-quadruplex containing DNA only and not in any other DNA samples. Also, such selective fluorescent recovery was not observed with chemically synthesized CD that lack hydroxyl functionality.

ce:section.ce:para: Repetitive G-rich structural Motifs in DNA fold to form stacked array of guanine tetrads which give rise to stable secondary structures known as G-quadruplex [1–5]. Selective in-vitro assay with polymerase extension and next generation sequencing claims the presence of more than 7,00,000 G-quadruplex-forming sequences in human genome [6]. G-quadruplex-forming sequences exist abundantly in the human gene promoters regions, immunoglobulin switch regions, as well as in telomeres [7–10]. Their presence in the telomere region protect cells from recombination and degradation [11]. Any disturbance in telomere maintenance is lethal to cell, which has been explored for development of cancer therapeutic agents. Reportedly, G-quadruplex has role in the regulation of transcription and genome stability [12,13]. A large array of small-molecule ligands are developed and screened to check their affinity for G-quadruplex to serve as ideal cancer therapeutics [14–18]. Active involvement of G-quadruplex in such a broad spectrum have drawn huge attention of researchers in interdisciplinary areas such as nucleic acid research, genomics, supramolecular chemistry, anticancer therapeutics and spectroscopy. Consequently, progress towards development of tools to easily identify G-quadruplex is of scientific interest which demands the development of simple strategies. Herein, a strategy for the visual detection of G-quadruplexes in nanomolar range DNA is demonstrated following a fluorescent on-off technique that involves a mushroom derived Carbon Dot (CD) in conjunction with Hemin as a selective G-quadruplex binding ligand.

Biophysical techniques have been extensively used for the characterization of G-quadruplex structures ex-vivo by using Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, Circular Dichroism Spectroscopy, and X-ray Crystallography [19–22]. Luminescent Ir(III) complexes has been explored for the selective detection of G-quadruplex motifs and subsequent label free detection of T4 polynucleotide kinase [23]. Platinum(II) complexes with specific ligands show significant increase in photoluminesence upon binding with G-quadruplex DNA [24]. Certain molecular machine based on Bisquinolinium derivatives has been designed that shows different affinity for G-quadruplex in free and metal bound state due to conformational change [25]. Dimethyl sulfate (DMS) footprinting and DNA polymerase arrest assays are traditional methods for G-quadruplex analysis conducted with radio-isotopic labeling and thin layer polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) [26]. Advancement in these traditional procedures for structural analysis of G-quadruplex-forming DNA sequences has been done by using fluorescent labels, capillary electrophoresis and primer extension methods [27,28]. Recently, fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy has been used for visualizing the interaction of G-quadruplex with ligands [29–31]. G-quadruplex detection in mammalian cell has been explored with the use of monoclonal antibodies [32]. However, the multistep tedious process, restriction of using radioactive tags in many labs worldwide and costly devices used in traditional methods limits quick detection of G Quadruplexes in DNA. Certain dyes like thioflavin T (ThT), triphenylmethane (TPM), thiazole orange (TO) and triphenylamine (TPA) dyes may serve as potent fluorescent probes for G-quadruplex detection by fluorescence enhancement [33–36]. Nevertheless, non-selective interaction of these fluorescent dyes with other forms of DNA along with G-quadruplexes often limit their applications.

Interestingly, non fluorescent cationic Manganese Porphyrin shows high selectivity towards G quadruplexes [37]. Iron (III) protoporphyrin IX (a positively charged 18 electron porphyrin), commonly known as Hemin, display significant selectivity towards G-Quadruplexes. Many peroxidase enzyme possess Hemin as the prosthetic group. DNAzyme based on G-quadruplex and Hemin mimics intrinsic peroxidase activity [38–41]. This property has been explored to develop colorimetric method of ligand screening for G-quadruplex [42]. G-quadruplex-Hemin DNAzyme has been also used for telomerase activity detection [43]. Luminol can be oxidized by H2O2 in presence of G-quadruplex/Hemin to generate chemiluminescence which further triggers CRET (chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer) to CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [44]. Elsewhere, high affinity of Hemin towards G-Quadruplex was taken advantage off to quench the fluorescence of Cd/Se Quantum dots that are covalently linked to G-quadruplex DNA sequence by electron transfer for detection of Adenosine mono phosphate (AMP) and single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) [45]. However, easier synthetic methods, readily available nontoxic substrate, comparable fluorescence and facile surface modification may prove CD as an attractive alternative to Quantum Dots for label free biosensing applications. In fact, Graphene CD and Hemin finds use in various biosensing platforms for detection of glucose, SNP and also serves as highly active biomimetic catalyst [46–48]. Hemin can be assembled on surfaces of CD through π–π stacking [46]. Herein, the high selectivity of Hemin towards G-quadruplex coupled with its ability to assemble on CD surface has been taken advantage off to design a strategy for visual and spectrophotometric detection of G-quadruplexes for the first time. Fluorescence of CD is quenched in the presence of Hemin; this quenched union is further used to detect G quadruplex by fluorescence recovery of CD. The fluorescence of Hemin/CD conjugate increase proportionately with increase in G quadruplex content in DNA. Since fluorescence recovery of Hemin/CD conjugate is selective for DNA sequences that form G quadruplexes, a label free, ultrasensitive, green and simple fluorescent biosensing platform was established for its detection. The significant property of this probe is that visual detection of the presence of G-quadruplex is possible in the nanoscale just with a handheld UV lamp that may have promising application prospects in the area of G-quadruplex research (Scheme 1 ).

Ganoderma lucidum is procured from NRCM (ICAR) Chambaghat, solan, Himachal Pradesh, India. Hemin, Sodium Hydroxide, Potassium Chloride, Agarose, Tris buffer, Acetic acid, Hydrogen Peroxide and Quinine Sulfate are procured from Sigma. Custom synthesized oligonucleotide was also purchased from Sigma. Hemin stock solution (10 mM) was prepared in Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) and stored at −20 °C in the dark.

Sporocarps of Ganoderma lucidum were washed with cold nanopure water and were completely dried at 50 °C. Afterwards, it was cut into smaller pieces and further crushed into a fine powder. In brief, 0.25 g Ganoderma lucidum powder was added into 4.5 mL ultrapure water at the beginning. After vigorous stirring for mixing, 500 μL of 30% H2O2 was mixed into it. Thereafter, this mixture was introduced into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 2 h, and naturally cooled down to room temperature. The resultant yellow colored aqueous solution was filtered with 0.2 μm PVDF membrane syringe filter to get rid of larger particles. This filtered product was adjusted to neutral pH with NaOH. Synthesis of CD was also carried out without addition of H2O2 as a control to evaluate the role of H2O2. The CD were stored at 4 °C in the dark condition for further characterization and found stable for about 3 months.

Characterization of the CD was done with fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV–visible spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM), Atomic Force Microscopic (AFM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Time-Resolved Fluorescence spectrophotometry as well as X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The fluorescence spectra are recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a slit width of both excitation and emission set at 5 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra are obtained on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). The morphology and size of the product are observed using a Tecnai G2, F30 HRTEM (FEI, Japan) at the accelerating potential of 300 KV. XRD was performed in a Rigaku TTRAX III X-ray diffractometer. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer spectrometer within a range of 400–4000 cm−1. XPS analysis was carried out on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe-II. Zeta potential of CD at neutral pH was measured using a Delsa Nano C (Beckman coulter, US). The fluorescence time resolved decays of CD alone, Hemin/CD conjugate as well as Hemin/CD conjugate with G-quadruplex were calculated by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer from Edinburgh Instruments (model: LifeSpec-II, UK) with single photon counting technique (TCSPC).

Hemin is non-covalently attached to CD via simple adsorption on the surface of CD to form Hemin/CD conjugate. ITC studies were conducted at 25 °C in 10 mM sodium cacodylate buffer containing 100 mM KCl at pH 7.0 using MicroCal ITC 200 (GE, USA). Concentration of CD was determined by performing agarose gel electrophoresis by comparison method with known DNA ladder (Fig. S1). Table 1 contains oligonucleotide used for G quadruplex preparation. The binding of Hemin to CD was determined by titrating 50 μM of CD against 200 μM of Hemin. Hemin binding with sequence 1 (VEGF sequence) was also evaluated in similar buffer condition. 20 μM of VEGF sequence in the sample cell were titrated against 200 μM of Hemin filled in the syringe.

The thermodynamic parameters were calculated using the equation:
ΔG = −RT ln Kd = ΔH° − TΔS

UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy was used to study the interaction between CD and Hemin molecules throughout the process of conjugation. The fluorescence titration study was conducted to calculate the LOD (limit of detection) of Hemin molecule by CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of G quadruplex and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: G-quadruplex was prepared by adding 5 nmol of VEGF oligonucleotide (sequence 1) in presence of 25 mM Tris-HCl 100 mM KCl solution in a final volume to 200 μL at neutral pH with nanopure water. This solution was kept at 37 °C overnight. Characterization and confirmation of G quadruplex formation were done with Circular Dichroism spectra. Circular Dichroism spectra of G-quadruplex was acquired on a Jasco J-1500. The Data were articulated as degrees of ellipticity, in units of millidegrees (mdeg). The sample was scanned thrice with a scan rate of 50 nm/ min using 1 mm pathlength cuvette. Circular Dichroism spectra was also acquired for 5 nmol of sequence 1 after addition of Hemin/CD conjugate (2 μL of 5 mg/ml CD conjugated with 5 μM Hemin solution).

DNA samples with or without G quadruplex was added to 2 mL of preincubated Hemin/CD conjugate solutions (2 μL of 5 mg/ml CD solution with 5 μM Hemin and 100 mM KCl). The solution was stirred vigorously and incubated at room temperature for 60 min in dark. Fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured with 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cells. The excitation wavelength was set at 330 nm. For visual detection, eppendorf tubes were held against hand held UV lamp or transilluminator and photographed with a canon D-60 digital camera.

CD was synthesized for the first time from Ganoderma lucidum. The presence of considerable amounts of polyphenols being one of the major driving force to use this mushroom as source of carbon for CD synthesis. CD has been synthesized by hydrothermal method, a bottom up approach. The oxidative treatment with H2O2 causes fragmentation of macroscopic carbon aggregates into nanosized particle and enhances its solubilization [49]. H2O2 breaks the π-π carbon bonds of carbon source to form carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on the surface of CD, thereby increasing the quantum yield [50]. The use of H2O2 improves the quantum yield as well as shorten the reaction time as shown previously also [51,52]. However, excess use of H2O2 (>500 μL/0.25 g of Ganoderma lucidum powder) decrease the quantum yield, most probably by uncontrolled etching of the CD surface and subsequent self-quenching. The current concentration of H2O2 used represent the optimum amount where fragmentation and solubility issues are taken care off during CD synthesis but excess etching is avoided. The total phenolic content of Ganoderma lucidum is ∼9.4 μg/g that provide hydroxyl group cover on surface of CD [53]. Presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CD makes it specific for interaction with Fe3+ and hence Hemin [54].

CD were characterized by a host of analytical methods. A broad band at approximately 26° with 0.34 nm d-spacing between the (002) planes was observed in powder XRD which reveals weak graphitic crystallinity (Fig. 1 A) [55]. HRTEM images displayed spherical and well dispersed CD with average 5 nm diameter of particle that increases slightly after conjugation with Hemin (Fig. S2). The well formed lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.34 nm were observed in HRTEM images (Fig. 1B). Lattice spacing was calculated with the help of Image J software that indicate (002) lattice facets of graphitic carbon. This lattice spacing as determined by HRTEM is in good agreement with the XRD data [55]. In FTIR spectra of CD, broad absorption bands at 3360 cm−1 and 3240 cm−1 are ascribed to OH and NH asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, characteristic absorption bands at 2940 cm−1 and 2635 cm−1 were assigned to CH stretching and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH, respectively. Bands at 1705 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 represent stretching vibrations of CO of carboxylic acid and tertiary amide respectively. The bands at 1395 cm−1 and 1208 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of CN and CN respectively. The FTIR results exhibited the presence of hydroxyl, amine and carboxyl groups, on the surface of the CD (Fig. 1C). Zeta potential was also measured for the CD to determine the charge and stability of colloidal dispersions. Zeta potential values of −6.71 mV indicate negative charge on the surface of CD (Fig. 1D).

Elemental analysis of CD was done by XPS spectra which also provide information about the surface state. XPS spectrum exhibits prominent peaks at 286.0, 401.6 and 533.1 eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The relative atomic percentages of these three elements are 77.77%, 2.50% and 19.67% respectively. This observation clearly suggests the integration of oxygen-rich moieties and doping with nitrogen in the CD. The spectrum of C1s exhibits three peaks at 283.26 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.58 eV, corresponding to the bonds of CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO respectively (Fig. 2B). The N1 s spectrum exhibits three fitted peaks at 402.2 eV, 401.59 eV and 399.34 eV that corresponds to alkyl ammonium CNH2, pyrrolic nitrogen (CNC) and pyridine nitrogen (CNC) respectively (Fig. 2C). The high resolution spectrum of O1 s is divided into two peaks, one positioned at 532.2 eV is ascribed to CO bonding of carbonyl group, while the other at 534.2 eV attributed to CO bonding (Fig. 2D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CD and Hemin/CD conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption at 240–290 nm in the UV spectra of CD is due to π-π* and n-π* electron transition of CC of carbonyl groups and pyridine nitrogen respectively (Fig. 3 A). Aqueous solution of CD gives bright blue emission under excitation with UV light and shows excitation dependent fluorescence emission. Excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CD is outcome of particle size distribution, different emissive trap sites and various emission centers (bright edge state) on similarly sized particles [56–61]. Maximum fluorescence emission peak was observed at 437 nm with a shoulder at ∼371 nm at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 3B). The shoulder, observed at ∼370 nm arise from the carbon-core, whereas the peak at ∼ 437 nm arises from the surface domain. The fluorescence resulting from the surface domain dominates compared to the fluorescence from the carbon core due to direct electron–hole recombination. 62 Gradual red-shifting of shoulder peak was observed with increase of excitation wavelength due to dependency of photoluminescence of CD on the former. Quinine sulphate (0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4), photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) std: 54% was used as a standard to calculate the PLQY of CD synthesized with H2O2 and without H2O2, which was found to be ∼20% and 6% respectively.

Hemin causes significant fluorescence quenching of CD. The fluorescence intensity of CD decreased with increasing concentration of Hemin (0.5–10 μM), but without any shift in peak position. Hemin shows good linear correlation of fluorescent quenching with CD in the concentration range 0.5–5 mM (Fig. 3C). However, this linear correlation was not followed beyond 5 μM of Hemin. This quenched union of Hemin and CD is further used in the downstream study to detect G quadruplex.

Plot of (F–F0) versus concentration of Hemin (C, μmol L−1) displays a straight line with linear regression equation ΔF = −36605 (C) + 99725 with R2 = 0.997. Limit of detection (LOD) of Hemin that must be present to quench CD fluorescence was found to be 330 nmolL−1 calculated from the relation: 3.3× σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation of the response (Fig. S3). Slight enhancement in fluorescence, accompanied by blue shift of emission peak was observed when Hemin without Fe3+, i.e. Protoporphyrin IX (concentration range 1–5 μM) and CD were mixed (Fig. S4). This result indicates that Fe3+ have a major role in quenching the fluorescence of CD by Hemin. The interaction of Hemin was also checked with citric acid derived CD which mainly possesses COOH group on the surface. No such fluorescence quenching was observed in this chemically synthesized CD with Hemin solution (Fig. S5). This justify the use of Ganoderma lucidum as a carbon source for synthesis of CD that present plenty of OH surface functionality required for non-covalent binding with Hemin.

The fluorescence lifetime of CD and CD/Hemin conjugate was calculated as 5.3 ns and 4.5 ns respectively. The decrease in fluorescence lifetime of CD/Hemin conjugate by 0.8 ns points towards a dynamic quenching process operative in the above mentioned concentration range due to diffusive collision between CD and Hemin (Fig. 3D) [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of thermodynamic parameters for interaction between CD and Hemin as well as sequence 1 (VEGF) and Hemin
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ ion of Hemin molecule interacts with hydroxyl functionality and other negative charge bearing ligands on the CD surface. Further, the porphyrin ring of Hemin interacts with the CD through π-π stacking interactions [46]. These are the main operating forces for non-covalent interaction of CD with Hemin. Noncovalent functionalization of CD is an elegant method that only involves surface functional groups without affecting the electronic network. The thermodynamic parameter of this union was evaluated with the help of ITC (Fig. 4 A). The binding constant was evaluated as Kb = 5.44 × 104 and dissociation constant Kd = 1.83 × 10−5. The corresponding value of ΔH = −5.9 Kcal/mol, ΔS = 1.81 cal/mol/deg and ΔG = −5.94 kcal/mol indicating enthalpically driven exothermic nature of the interaction. The binding affinity of G quadruplex (in sequence 1, VEGF) with Hemin was also evaluated with ITC. The binding constant (Kb) was found to be 1.14 × 105 for G-quadruplex and Hemin interaction and corresponding ΔH = −114 Kcal/mol, ΔS = −360 cal/mol/deg and ΔG = −105.00 kcal/mol (Fig. 4B). Evidently, stronger binding was observed in the case of G-quadruplex with Hemin in comparison to Hemin with CD [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of G-quadruplex with Hemin/CD conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: Sequence 1 is specifically present in VEGF (Vascular endothelial growth factor) promoter region and ligand mediated G quadruplex stabilization of this promoter gene is done for modulating transcription [65,66]. Sequence 2 is telomere sequence that is present on each end of chromosome [13]. In G-quadruplex, four Guanines are arranged in a rotationally symmetric manner to form hydrogen bonds around the corners resulting in planar G-quartet structures. These planar G-quartets are stabilized by K+ ions which interact with the oxygen lone pairs of Guanine bases [67]. The characteristic negative peak at 240 nm and positive peak at 265 nm in Circular Dichroism spectra corresponds to intramolecular parallel type of G quadruplex characteristic of the G quadruplex forming sequence (VEGF sequence) used in the present study (Fig. S6). In case of VEGF sequence, intensity of positive peak at 265 nm increases in the presence of Hemin and 100 mM K+ ions that show that Hemin molecule possesses intrinsic affinity for G quadruplex and has a stabilizing effect. Interestingly, increase in the peak intensity of characteristic G-quadruplex peak with CD/Hemin conjugate was observed that reveals that Hemin stripped off from CD and stabilizes G quadruplex.

Digital images of tube containing CD alone, Hemin/CD conjugate and Hemin/CD conjugate in presence of G-quadruplex under UV light shows visible recovery of CD fluorescence after incubation with G-Quadruplexes (Fig. 5 A) while under daylight all solution looks similar (Fig. S7). Fluorescence of CD was quenched after conjugation with Hemin with LOD of 330 nmolL−1. Emission intensity of Hemin/CD conjugate was enhanced gradually with increasing concentration of sequence 1 having G quadruplex (Fig. 5B). A minimum of 8 nmol (∼4 μM/L) of sequence 1 (VEGF) that harbor G-quadruplex was essential to restore the original fluorescence of CD. The plot of (F-F0) versus concentration of G quadruplex (C, μM) displays a straight line with linear regression equation of ΔF = −49872 (C) + 10291 with R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 5C). The limit of detection of G quadruplex in sequence 1 was found to be ∼189 nmol L−1. The selectivity of the sensing mechanism for G-Quadruplex present in sequence 1 (VEGF sequence) were determined by performing the test in a series of control DNA samples that lack G-quadruplex like calf thymus DNA (ctDNA), herring sperm DNA (hsDNA) and pUC 19 plasmid (Fig. S8). Negligible recovery of CD emission was observed in the control samples.

An increase of 0.3 ns in fluorescence lifetime of Hemin/CD conjugate was observed upon its interaction with G quadruplex sequence (sequence 1) (Fig. 5D). This increase in fluorescence life time indicate efficient deshielding of CD from Hemin. Fluorescence recovery of Hemin/CD conjugate was also checked with sequence 2, the well-known G-quadruplex telomere sequence and found to be almost similar (Fig. S9).

Neither dsDNA control samples nor G quadruplex sequences (sequence 1) were able to interact with CD efficiently that is evident from negligible changes in CD fluorescence in presence of all the DNA samples including sequence 1 (Figs. S10 and S11). Net charge possessed by CD as well as dsDNA control samples is negative, which limits interaction between them and indicate the incompetence of CD to detect G quadruplex sequence alone. Hemin/CD conjugate dissociates and Hemin preferably slides inside the G quadruplex (sequence 1) that subsequently result in deshielding of CD leading to its fluorescence recovery. It is well known that Hemin has inherent affinity for G quadruplex. Once inside the G-quadruplex, the Hemin fails to quench the fluorescence of CD and the fluorescence of CD revives. However, insignificant change in the emission intensity of Hemin/CD conjugate was observed in the presence of control DNA samples of ctDNA, hsDNA and plasmid (pUC 19). Thus, the methodology described herein could potentially be used to detect of the presence of G-quadruplexes having Hoogsteen hydrogen bonding irrespective of the DNA source and the fluorescent outcome clearly distinguishes them from normal DNA with Watson and Crick base pairing.

A novel CD was prepared from the oriental mushroom Ganoderma lucidum following hydrothermal process. The CD was well characterized with various analytical techniques that ascertain presence of hydroxyl functionalty on the surface among others. Noncovalent functionalization of CD with Hemin was achieved to create a quenched union that provides the probe to intercept the presence of G-quadruplexes in DNA sequences. The inherent affinity of Hemin towards G-quadruplexes make them to slide inside G-quadruplexes dissociating the Hemin/CD complex. The quenched union of Hemin/CD fluoresces back after being stripped off Hemin from the CD surface. On the basis of these unique properties of this Hemin/CD conjugate, a novel assay for G quadruplex detection with LOD of ∼190 nmol L−1 was achieved. This assay is simple, rapid, cost-efficient, and there is no need to label DNA substrates. The most critical feature of the assay is that, direct visualization of the G quadruplexin nanomolar range DNA is possible through a hand held UV lamp at room temperature. We foresee that the suggested mechanism and detection strategy is endowed with the capability to serve as a biosensor to sense G quadruplex with the potential to design point-of-care format.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Indian Institute of Technology Mumbai for XPS and HRTEM analysis. This research has been financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology Patna (IITP). B.K acknowledges Indian Institute of Technology Patna for fellowship. There is no conflict of interest in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2018.05.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability and kinetics of G-quadruplex structures
Quadruplex DNA: sequence, topology and structure
G-quadruplex DNA structures variations on a theme
Highly prevalent putative quadruplex sequence motifs in human DNA
Crystal structure of parallel quadruplexes from human telomeric DNA
High-throughput sequencing of DNA G-quadruplex structures in the human genome
G-quadruplexes in promoters throughout the human genome
Stability and kinetics of c-MYC promoter G-quadruplexes studied by single-molecule manipulation
Structural analysis of G-quadruplex formation at the human MEST promoter
Mechanism and regulation of class switch recombination
G-quadruplex nucleic acids and human disease
G-quadruplexes and their regulatory roles in biology
DNA secondary structures: stability and function of G-quadruplex structures
G-quadruplexes in the human genome: detection, functions and therapeutic potential
Targeting G-quadruplexes in gene promoters: a novel anticancer strategy?
Assessment of gene promoter G-quadruplex binding and modulation by a naphthalene diimide derivative in tumor cells
Exploring the formation and recognition of an important G-quadruplex in a HIF1 α promoter and its transcriptional inhibition by a benzo[c]phenanthridine derivative
Stabilization of novel immunoglobulin switch regions G-quadruplexes by naphthalene and quinoline-based ligands
Structure of a left-handed DNA G-quadruplex
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
The high-resolution crystal structure of a parallel intermolecular DNA G-4 quadruplex/drug complex employing syn glycosyl linkages
Structure of human telomeric RNA (TERRA): stacking of two G-quadruplex blocks in K+ solution
Bulges in G-quadruplexes: broadening the definition of G-quadruplex-forming sequences
Label-free luminescence switch-on detection of T4 polynucleotide kinase activity using a G quadruplex-selective probe
Platinum(II) complexes with dipyridophenazine ligands as human telomerase inhibitors and luminescent probes for G-quadruplex DNA
A metal-mediated conformational switch controls G-quadruplex binding affinity
Biochemical techniques for the characterization of G-quadruplex structures: EMSA, DMS footprinting, and DNA polymerase stop assay
Fluorescence methods for probing G-quadruplex structure in single and double-stranded DNA
Detection of G-quadruplex DNA using primer extension as a tool
